planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade with protection from hot afternoon sun. Silky Camellia is slow to establish and resents transplanting; choose the permanent location carefully and amend the soil deeply before planting.

directions: Silky Camellia is widely regarded as one of the most beautiful native shrubs in North America, native to moist, acidic forest understories from Virginia and Tennessee south to Florida and west to Arkansas and Texas, where it blooms in May through July with large, pure white, five-petaled flowers featuring a central boss of deep bluish-purple filaments with yellow anthers — a combination found nowhere else in the native flora and reminiscent of a Japanese camellia. The flowers open sequentially over several weeks and are followed by attractive woody capsules; the fall foliage turns shades of orange and scarlet. Despite its beauty, Silky Camellia is notoriously slow-growing and slow to establish, taking many years to reach blooming size and resenting transplanting once established — but gardeners who invest the time and proper siting conditions are rewarded with a genuinely spectacular flowering shrub. Provide moist, deeply amended, acidic soil in partial shade with protection from hot afternoon sun; mulch heavily and water regularly during the first several years.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade. Mountain Camellia is somewhat easier to establish than Silky Camellia (Stewartia malacodendron) and more cold-hardy; avoid waterlogged or alkaline soils.

directions: Mountain Camellia is a striking native understory shrub or small tree, native to rich, moist, wooded slopes, ravine walls, and stream terraces in the southern Appalachians from Virginia and Kentucky south to Georgia and Alabama, blooming in July and August with large, white, five-petaled flowers bearing a prominent cluster of orange-tipped stamens. Larger than Silky Camellia (Stewartia malacodendron) and more cold-hardy (to zone 5), Mountain Camellia blooms later in summer when few other native shrubs are flowering, extending ornamental interest deep into the season. The smooth, exfoliating gray bark provides attractive winter interest, and the foliage turns shades of orange and red in fall. Grow in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — it is somewhat more tolerant of garden conditions than Silky Camellia and establishes more readily from nursery transplants, though it is still slow-growing and long-lived.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Two-winged Silverbell is more heat-tolerant than Carolina Silverbell (Halesia carolina) and is the better choice for Gulf Coast gardens in zones 7–9.

directions: Two-winged Silverbell is a beautiful native understory tree, native to the rich, moist bottomland forests and floodplain terraces of the southeastern coastal plain from North Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, where it blooms prolifically in March and April with cascading clusters of white, bell-shaped flowers that cover the bare branches before the leaves emerge — one of the finest native spring-flowering trees in the southern garden. Distinguished from Carolina Silverbell (Halesia carolina) by its fruit having only two wings instead of four, and adapted to the warmer, more humid conditions of the lower South, Two-winged Silverbell is the appropriate silverbell for Gulf Coast gardens. The bright green summer foliage, attractive bark, and yellow fall color provide year-round interest; grow in moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade under larger canopy trees or at woodland edges. More tolerant of summer heat than H. carolina.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade. Yellowwood is a long-lived, low-maintenance native shade tree that does not bloom every year — heavy flowering is often biennial or triggered by summer drought stress.

directions: American Yellowwood is a superb native shade tree, native to rich, well-drained limestone bluffs, rocky slopes, and bottomland forest edges in the mid-South from North Carolina and Kentucky south to Alabama and west to Oklahoma, producing in May and June the most spectacular flowering display of any native shade tree — long, pendulous clusters of fragrant, white, pea-like flowers resembling wisteria that drape the wide-spreading canopy in a breathtaking display. The smooth, light gray bark resembles beech bark and is ornamental year-round; the bright green, pinnately compound leaves turn yellow in fall; and the wood at the heartwood core is bright yellow — hence the common name. Yellowwood blooms on alternate years or following stress, but the wait is always worth it. Grow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; it adapts to a wide range of conditions once established and is a long-lived, virtually pest-free tree.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants (male and female plants together for berry production) in spring in wet to moist, acidic, sandy to peaty soil in partial shade to full sun. Large Gallberry is adapted to the wet pine flatwoods and pocosins of the Gulf Coast and coastal plain.

directions: Large Gallberry is a robust evergreen native holly, native to the wet pine flatwoods, pocosins, bay swamps, and seepage bogs of the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Texas, where it grows alongside inkberry, fetterbush, and pond cypress in permanently moist to wet, highly acidic, sandy or peaty soils. The glossy, dark green, leathery, spine-tipped leaves provide year-round evergreen structure; female plants produce abundant clusters of large black berries in fall and winter that are critical food for bluebirds, hermit thrushes, and other migrating songbirds. Like inkberry (Ilex glabra), Large Gallberry is important for native bees, producing copious nectar that supports early-season bee populations. Plant male and female specimens together for reliable fruiting; provide consistently moist to wet, highly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun. An outstanding wildlife shrub for wet native gardens in the lower South.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade to full sun. Large Fothergilla tolerates partial shade but produces the best fall color and most flowers in full sun with consistent moisture.

directions: Large Fothergilla is an exceptional four-season native shrub, native to the mountain forests and rocky slopes of the southern Appalachians from Virginia and North Carolina south to Alabama, producing fragrant, white, bottlebrush-like flower spikes of dense stamens in April and May before the leaves emerge — a display that rivals those of its close relative Witch Hazel (Hamamelis). Larger than Dwarf Fothergilla (F. gardenii), it grows 6–10 feet tall and wide and provides multi-season appeal through its dark green, blue-green tinged summer foliage, one of the finest fall color displays of any native shrub (crimson, orange, and yellow often appearing simultaneously on a single leaf), and attractive winter form. Grow in moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; best fall color develops in full sun, but the plant tolerates partial shade and is easy to establish in typical native plant garden conditions. An outstanding choice for four-season structure in the southeastern native garden.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, highly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade. Rusty Staggerbush is adapted to the dry, scrubby flatwoods and ridge habitats of the Florida peninsula and coastal plain, and requires excellent drainage to thrive.

directions: Rusty Staggerbush is a coarse-textured evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the dry, sandy, scrubby flatwoods, pine ridges, and xeric hammocks of the Florida peninsula and coastal plain from South Carolina to Alabama, distinguished by its large, leathery leaves with rust-colored (ferruginous) scales on the undersides that give the species its common name. In spring it produces clusters of white to pale pink, urn-shaped flowers typical of the heath family (Ericaceae), which are important nectar sources for native bees; the plant may grow 8–20 feet tall in ideal conditions, forming small trees in open scrub habitats. All parts are toxic if ingested by livestock — the 'staggerbush' name refers to the neurological symptoms caused in cattle that graze the foliage. Grow in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; it is drought-tolerant once established and an important component of Florida scrub restoration plantings.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Southern Maidenhair Fern is unusual among native ferns in preferring neutral to alkaline soil and often grows naturally on moist limestone outcrops, seep ledges, and waterfall spray zones.

directions: Southern Maidenhair Fern is the subtropical maidenhair native to the Gulf Coast and southern United States, native to moist limestone ledges, cave mouths, waterfall spray zones, and moist shaded cliffs from Virginia to Florida and west to California and south through the tropics, where it grows in the constant moisture of seeping or spray-wetted rock faces in deep shade. Its delicate, fan-shaped, bright green leaflets on arching, wiry black stems make it one of the most graceful and ornamental of all ferns; in zones 8–11 it is semi-evergreen to evergreen in the garden, providing year-round texture in the deep shade. Unlike most native ferns, Southern Maidenhair Fern prefers neutral to slightly alkaline soil and is naturally found on limestone substrates — amend planting areas with crushed limestone or dolomite and keep the soil constantly moist. An exceptional fern for shaded water features, seep gardens, or moist rockeries.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade. Mountain Sweet Pepperbush is more shade-tolerant and grows taller than its coastal relative (Clethra alnifolia) and is better suited to mountain and Piedmont gardens in zones 5–8.

directions: Mountain Sweet Pepperbush is a tall native understory shrub, native to the rich, moist, hardwood forests and mountain cove edges of the southern Appalachians from Virginia and North Carolina south to Georgia and Alabama, growing to 12 feet or more in deep, humus-rich mountain soils and producing fragrant white flower spikes in July and August that attract bumblebees and native butterflies. Distinguished from its coastal relative (Clethra alnifolia) by its greater height, more shade tolerance, and spectacular cinnamon-brown exfoliating bark that peels away in shaggy layers through the winter — an outstanding ornamental feature that rivals the most prized native bark textures. An excellent shrub for the shade garden in zones 5–8, where it tolerates conditions that defeat many other flowering shrubs; grow in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade and mulch deeply with leaf litter to maintain consistent moisture.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated transplants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, acidic, sandy soil in partial shade to full sun. Georgia Plume is critically rare in the wild and should only be sourced from specialty native plant nurseries; the species rarely produces viable seed and is most effectively propagated by root cuttings.

directions: Georgia Plume is one of the rarest and most endangered native shrubs in the United States, a monotypic genus (the only species in its family in North America) endemic to the Sandhills and longleaf pine region of Georgia, where fewer than 50 wild populations survive in dry, sandy, acidic longleaf pine savannas and turkey oak ridges. In July it produces spectacular terminal plumes of small white, four-petaled flowers in drooping clusters 6–12 inches long — an arresting display on a plant that looks unlike anything else in the native flora. The species rarely sets viable seed in the wild (likely due to the collapse of its pollinator community), and natural populations reproduce almost entirely by root sprouting; specialty native plant nurseries propagate it by root cuttings. Grow in dry to moist, well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in partial to full sun — it is intolerant of heavy, wet, or alkaline soils. Supporting cultivation of Georgia Plume in private gardens is an important conservation act.
